banner: Week 31 · Prove It — AA Similarity & Proportions
essential_question: Why is matching just TWO angles enough to guarantee two triangles are similar — and can you set up and solve a proportion for a missing side under pressure?
objective: Justify why the AA Similarity Postulate works (the third angle is forced by the 180° angle sum), set up and solve proportions to find missing sides and scale factors, review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the mastery checkpoint on similar triangles and AA similarity.

content.prompt: Without looking at your notes, sketch two triangles that share only two marked congruent angles — nothing else marked. Label the third angle of each using the 180° rule, and circle whether the two triangles must be similar, must be congruent, or neither.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 27A & 27B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 27A/27B. We check the tricky ones: completing two-column AA-similarity proofs where perpendicular segments force a shared right angle, or parallel lines force congruent angles across a transversal; and the 'corresponding parts of similar figures are proportional' problems — including the one where X is the TOTAL base of the larger triangle, not just the extra piece, which trips people up. Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: two triangles share only two pairs of congruent angles — nobody checked the third pair or any side. Argue why they must ALSO have a congruent third angle pair, using nothing but the 180° angle sum.

Claim 2: similar triangles are NOT necessarily congruent. Argue why 'same shape' doesn't force 'same size,' and give a scale factor other than 1 that would make two similar triangles clearly not congruent.

Claim 3: given a proportion built from two similar triangles' corresponding sides (like a/b = c/d), argue why cross-multiplying is valid, and use it to explain how you can solve for one missing side even when you're only given three of the four side lengths.

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 28 — Transformations: Translations, Reflections & Rotations — where instead of same-shape/different-size, you'll explore moves that preserve a figure exactly on the coordinate plane.

success_criteria.text: I can state the definition of similar triangles and the AA Similarity Postulate without help
I can justify why matching two angle pairs forces the third pair to match, and why that makes AA sufficient for similarity
I can set up and solve a proportion to find a missing side or a scale factor between similar triangles

options: all corresponding angles are congruent AND all corresponding sides are proportional
only the angles match; the side ratios can differ from side to side
they are the exact same size
questions.answer: all corresponding angles are congruent AND all corresponding sides are proportional
questions.explain: Similarity is a two-part promise — congruent angles, and sides that all share ONE constant ratio (the scale factor) — not a vague 'looks the same' impression.

questions.prompt: The AA Similarity Postulate says that if two angles of one triangle are congruent to two angles of another triangle, the two triangles are…
questions.explain: Two matching angle pairs force the third pair to match too (the 180° angle sum leaves no other option), which is exactly why AA alone guarantees similarity.

questions.prompt: Two similar right triangles: the smaller has a leg of 8 and a hypotenuse of X; the larger, similar triangle has the corresponding leg 12 and the corresponding hypotenuse 15. Find X.
questions.answer: Scale factor from the larger to the smaller is 8 ÷ 12 = 2/3. X = 15 × (2/3) = 10.
questions.explain: Set up the proportion 8/12 = X/15, cross-multiply (8 × 15 = 12 × X), and solve: 120 = 12X, so X = 10.

questions.prompt: On the grid, draw two similar (but NOT congruent) triangles with a scale factor of your own choosing (not 1). Mark the two pairs of angles you use to justify the similarity by AA.
questions.answer: Two same-shape, clearly different-size triangles, with two pairs of angles arc-marked alike (matching style per corresponding pair).
questions.explain: Rubric: two clearly similar, non-congruent triangles drawn (1 pt) + two pairs of corresponding angles correctly and consistently arc-marked (1 pt).

questions.prompt: △RST ~ △UVW, with a scale factor from △RST to △UVW of 3. If RS = 4.5, what is UV? If VW = 21, what is ST?
questions.explain: Multiply by the scale factor to go from the smaller triangle's side to the larger triangle's corresponding side; divide by it to go the other way.

questions.prompt: Complete the proof. Given: AB⊥BD and EC⊥BD, with E on segment AD and C on segment BD (so △ECD sits inside △ABD, sharing ∠D). Prove: △ABD ~ △ECD. Statements: (1) AB⊥BD and EC⊥BD. (2) ∠ABD and ∠ECD are right angles. (3) ∠ABD ≅ ∠ECD. (4) ∠D ≅ ∠D. (5) △ABD ~ △ECD. Fill in the REASON for each of the five statements above.
questions.answer: (1) Given. (2) Definition of perpendicular lines. (3) All right angles are congruent. (4) Reflexive Property. (5) AA Similarity Postulate — ∠ABD ≅ ∠ECD and the shared ∠D give two congruent angle pairs between the two triangles.
questions.explain: This is the classic AA-similarity setup: two perpendicular segments force a shared right angle, and a shared vertex angle (Reflexive Property) supplies the second angle pair — together, exactly enough for AA.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 27 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea — AA similarity, scale factor, or solving proportions — you'd want to revisit before Lesson 28's transformations.
